rulings.Case Title: JUAN LOPEZ VS. MAMA MIA PIZZERIA et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Setting Aside And Vacating Default And Default Judgment And Granting Defts Leave To Defend
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 24, 2013, Line 7, DEFENDANTs MAMA MIA PIZZERIA, ARTEM EMMANUILOV and AGABIE EMMANUILOV's Motion For Order Setting Aside And Vacating Default And Default Judgment And Granting Defendants Leave To Defend. Denied without prejudice. Defendants have not complied with Labor Code § 98(f) prior to filing this motion. In his letter dated February 25, 2013, Defendants' counsel requested a stay of execution, which the Labor Commissioner denied. (Redmond Decl., Exhs. K, L.) Defendants did not apply for relief pursuant Labor Code 98(f). "'[T]he word "relief" [in the last sentence of section 98(f)] clearly refers to relief from such failure to either answer or appear' in the administrative proceeding." (Gonzalez v. Beck (2007) 158 Cal.App.4th 598, 605.) Further, "[i]t is apparent from the language of section 98(f) that the Legislature intended to require employers who do not answer or appear in an administrative wage claim proceeding, and who thereafter do not timely appeal from a decision of the Commissioner, to resort to the specific administrative remedy for relief in accordance with Code of Civil Procedure section 473 as a prerequisite to judicial action." Id. at 607. The Labor Commissioner's denial of a stay of execution did not render Defendants' exhaustion of administrative remedies futile. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/MJM)

rulings.Case Title: ALLISON JOHNSON VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al*****CASE TRANSFERRED TO SAN MATEO COUNTY*****
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Allison Johnson, By And Through Her Guardian Ad Litem'S Motion To Compel Further Responses From Deft County Of San Mateo To Six Form Interrogatories And All Requests For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, May 24, 2013, Line 2, Plaintiff Allison Johnson, By And Through Her Guardian Ad Litem's Motion To Compel Further Responses From Defendant County Of San Mateo To Six Form Interrogatories And All Requests For Production Of Documents Pro Tem Judge David McDonald, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem has not issued a tentative ruling on this motion. Each party is required to bring a proposed order to the hearing. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: AVERY MROZINSKI, BY HER GUARDIAN AD LITEM, TODD et al VS. OLD NAVY, LLC, A DELAWARE CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Strike Punitive Damages
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 24, 2013, Line 14, DEFENDANT OLD NAVY, LLC's motion To Strike Punitive Damages. Hearing required. Counsel are requested to be prepared to address the following questions. 1. What is Michigan's specific interest in having its law of punitive damages apply to this case? What specific case authority supports this specific position? Is there any dispute that there is no legal basis for punitive damages under Michigan law? 2. What is California's interest in having its punitive damages law applied here? Is it significant that punitive damages are sought for negligence as well as for premises liability? 3. Is the domicile or residence of the plaintiff relevant to the choice of law issue here? In re Disaster at Detroit Metro Airport, 750 F. Supp 793, 805 (E.D. Mich. 1989) suggests not. ("the domicile or residence of the plaintiff is not relevant to an evaluation of the choice of law issues concerning punitive damages because the decision by a state on whether to allow punitive damages focuses solely on corporate regulatory versus corporate protective policies"). 4. Hill v. Novartis Pharm. Corp., 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 38516 states at *28-*29 that California recognizes "with respect to the issue of punitive damages, 'the place of the wrong has the predominant interest,''' and that "'California considers the 'place of the wrong' to be the state where the last event necessary to make the actor liabile occurred.'" (Citations omitted.) What was the last event necessary to allegedly make Defendant liable here? If it occurred in Michigan, does Michigan have the greater interest here? =(302/MJM)
